title: "I oppose abortion, but there are procedures bills have to follow"
author: James Silberman
orientation: Silberman dismantles the procedural excuse Oklahoma senators used to kill SB13 (The Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act), showing both that no Senate rule was actually being violated by Senator Silk's motion and that even if a rule had to be suspended, elevating procedure over the lives of preborn children is morally indefensible.
audiences: Oklahoma constituents pressing their state senators
Pastors and Christians weighing legislative ethics
Republican legislators who claim to oppose abortion
Activists confronting officials with procedural objections

questions.question: What is the recurring excuse Oklahoma abolitionists hear from senators who voted to kill SB13?
questions.audience: Oklahoma constituents pressing their state senators
questions.answer: Senators repeatedly fall back on variations of a procedural defense, claiming their vote was not about opposing abolition itself but about protecting legislative process and order.
questions.quote: One of the most common excuses Oklahoma abolitionists hear from Senators who voted to kill The Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act is a version of “I oppose abortion, but there are procedures bills have to follow,” or “there are legislative norms we have to keep,” or “there’s a process we have to respect.”

questions.question: How did Senator Larry Boggs frame the procedural argument at the Haskell County GOP meeting?
questions.audience: Activists confronting officials with procedural objections
questions.answer: Boggs publicly insisted every bill, including SB13, must follow the same procedure or chaos would result, while simultaneously calling abortion an abomination, exposing the hierarchy of priorities he was actually defending.
questions.quote: “There is a process that not just Senate Bill 13 must follow, but every bill filed must follow,” Boggs said. “And if we don’t – listen – we will have total chaos… Abortion is an abomination to me, but there’s a procedure that we must follow.”

questions.question: Did Senator Joseph Silk's motion to withdraw SB13 from committee actually violate Senate rules?
questions.answer: No. Senate Rule 7-9 explicitly authorizes withdrawing any bill from committee on a two-thirds vote and placing it on General Order, so the motion was fully in line with established procedure.
questions.quote: This motion was fully in accordance with [Oklahoma Senate Rule 7-9](https://oksenate.gov/senate-rules#l-rule-7-9) which states, “Any bill or resolution may be withdrawn from any committee of the Senate upon a two-thirds vote of the members of the Senate. Any bill or resolution so withdrawn shall be on General Order.” There is absolutely nothing chaotic or violative of procedures about the motion.

questions.question: Is the claim that SB13 needed a House coauthor before being placed on General Order accurate?
questions.audience: Republican legislators who claim to oppose abortion
questions.answer: It is misleading. The March 11 vote was only to place SB13 on General Order, and bills routinely advance to General Order without yet having a House coauthor.
questions.quote: The March 11 vote was merely whether to place SB13 on General Order, and there is no Senate rule against a bill being on General Order without a house coauthor. It is not at all uncommon for a bill to pass through committee and be placed on General Order before it has a House coauthor.

questions.question: Even if a rule had to be suspended, what is Silberman's deeper moral objection to the procedure defense?
questions.audience: Pastors and Christians weighing legislative ethics
questions.answer: Treating procedural norms as a sufficient reason not to establish justice for image-bearing children is a heinous misordering of priorities, regardless of whether the rules were actually being broken.
questions.quote: But what’s more grievous than the dishonesty about whether voting for SB13 would have required the suspending of rules is the suggestion that abolishing abortion isn’t worth suspending a rule or two or however many it’s necessary to suspend. To hide behind procedure as an excuse for not establishing justice, even if procedures were being violated, is a heinous misordering of priorities.

questions.question: How did Senate Floor Leader Kim David justify killing SB13, and how does the article frame that justification before God?
questions.audience: Pastors and Christians weighing legislative ethics
questions.answer: Kim David explicitly prioritized preserving the Senate's committee process while she was in charge, which Silberman portrays as choosing institutional norms over the lives of human image-bearers.
questions.quote:
Senator Floor Leader Kim David told News 9, “I mean the history of the Senate is that bills work through the process through committee and I want that process to be able to continue as long as I’m in charge of the floor.”

Imagine standing before God and giving as your explanation for your motion to prevent abortion’s abolition that you cared more about wanting “that process to be able to continue” than you did about ending the slaughter of image bearers

questions.question: What concrete response does Silberman urge constituents to give when a senator hides behind procedure?
questions.audience: Activists confronting officials with procedural objections
questions.answer: Cite Oklahoma Senate Rule 7-9 directly to refute the false procedural claim, and then press the larger moral point that even genuine rule-suspension would be justified to abolish abortion.
questions.quote: If any Senator gives you this excuse, cite Senate Rule 7-9 to them and explain that even if abolishing abortion requires the suspending of a rule, that it would be evil not to.

related.title: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
related.reason: Silberman's foundational case for SB13 as the only acceptable Oklahoma abolition vehicle, providing the strategic context this article presupposes.

related.title: Anti-SB13 Campaigners Aren't Ignorant. They're Trying to Deceive You.
related.reason: Parallel argument that opposition messaging against SB13 is dishonest, not merely uninformed, mirroring this piece's charge that senators are knowingly misrepresenting Senate rules.

related.title: Responding to Larry Boggs' Explanation of Tuesday's Confrontation
related.reason: Direct companion piece on the same Larry Boggs episode that this article references via the Haskell County GOP meeting.

related.title: Contrary to Treat's Media Tour, SB13 is Not a Secession Bill
related.reason: Another rebuttal to a misleading procedural/legal objection raised by Senate leadership against SB13.

related.title: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
related.reason: Names another senior Oklahoma Senate leader using procedural cover to block SB13, extending this article's accusation beyond Boggs and David.

related.title: Senator McCortney Again Denies SB13 a Fair Hearing
related.reason: Concrete example of a self-described pro-life senator using procedural maneuvering to keep SB13 from a vote.

related.title: Motion to Bring SB13 to Senate Floor Coming Tomorrow! Email Your Senator!
related.reason: Action alert for the very March 11 motion under Senate Rule 7-9 that this article analyzes after the fact.
